fields.name: C Programming
fields.description: The primary goal of this course is to provide students with a solid foundation in the principles of programming and to impart practical skills in the C programming language. This course ensures that students comprehend the fundamental concepts of variables, data types, control structures, and functions within the context of C. Advanced topics such as pointers, structures, file handling and the standard C library are explored to broaden students' programming capabilities. Also, through project-based assessments and evaluations, students apply their knowledge to real-world scenarios, fostering creativity and project development skills.

fields.name: Foundation of DataScience (BCT)
fields.description: The objective of this course is to introduce the core concepts, tools, and methodologies of data science, focusing on the tools and techniques needed to analyze and interpret data effectively. Using data science tools, students will cover the entire data science process, from data acquisition, data manipulation, visualization, probability, statistics, and machine learning, with applications in business and engineering.

fields.name: Computer Organization & Architecture (BCT)
fields.description: The objective of this course is to provide the organization and architectural concept of computer system including processor architecture, computer arithmetic, memory system, I/O organization, multiprocessor and multicore.

fields.name: Computer Organization & Architecture (BEI)
fields.description: The objective of this course is to provide the organization, architecture and designing concept of computer system including processor architecture, computer arithmetic, memory system, I/O organization and multicore.

fields.name: Design & Analysis of Algorithm (CSIT)
fields.description: This course introduces basic elements of the design and analysis of computer algorithms. Topics include asymptotic notations and analysis, divide and conquer strategy, greedy methods, dynamic programming, basic graph algorithms, NP-completeness, and approximation algorithms. For each topic, beside in-depth coverage, one or more representative problems and their algorithms shall be discussed.

fields.name: Artificial Intelligence (BEI)
fields.description: The objective of this course is to build a strong foundation in Artificial Intelligence (AI), covering intelligent agents, search techniques, knowledge representation, machine and learning. It aims to equip students with both theoretical knowledge and practical skills to apply AI techniques in solving real-world problems, while also fostering awareness of the ethical considerations associated with AI applications.

fields.name: OOP in Python (BEL)
fields.description: This course gives overview about the programming paradigms and focuses on multi- paradigm programming model Python. This course introduces with the semantics of Python programming language and helps learners to master the skills of basic Python programming and object-oriented programming. It also highlights why Python is a useful scripting language for all developers.

fields.name: Artificial Intelligence
fields.description: The objective of this course is to build a strong foundation in Artificial Intelligence (AI), covering intelligent agents, search techniques, knowledge representation, machine and learning. It aims to equip students with both theoretical knowledge and practical skills to apply AI techniques in solving real-world problems, while also fostering awareness of the ethical considerations associated with AI applications.

fields.name: Introduction
fields.description:
1.1 Definition, foundation, history of AI
1.2 AI tree: Branches and interdisciplinary nature
1.3 Definition and importance of knowledge and learning
1.4 Human intelligence and machine intelligence
1.5 Intelligent agents and types

fields.name: Problem Solving and Search
fields.description:
2.1 Formal problem definition: States, actions, transitions, well-defined problems
2.2 Constraint satisfaction problems: Node consistency, path consistency, backtracking
2.3 Search algorithms, strategies, and evaluations
2.4 Uninformed: BFS, DFS, iterative deepening
2.5 Informed search: Best first search, greedy search, A* algorithm
2.6 Adversarial search: Minimax algorithm, alpha-beta pruning
2.7 Local search and optimization: Hill climbing, simulated annealing
2.8 Evolutionary optimization: Genetic algorithm

fields.name: Knowledge Representation and Probabilistic Reasoning
fields.description:
3.1 Knowledge-based agent
3.2 Knowledge representation techniques and issues in representation
3.3 Propositional and predicate logic
3.4 Semantic networks and frames
3.5 Probabilistic reasoning, inference using Bayes' theorem
3.6 Fuzzy logic: Membership functions, fuzzy inference systems

fields.name: Machine Learning Fundamentals
fields.description:
4.1 Forms of learning: Supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, reinforcement learning
4.2 Machine learning pipeline
4.2.1 Preprocessing and cleaning
4.2.2 Model development
4.2.3 Training, testing, and hyperparameter tuning
4.3 Overfitting, underfitting, bias-variance tradeoff
4.4 Regression and classification with linear model
4.5 Classification using decision tree and Naive Bayes
4.6 Evaluation with confusion matrix

fields.name: Neural Networks and Deep Learning Algorithms
fields.description:
5.1 Neural networks: Structures and activation functions
5.2 Perceptron, multilayer perceptron, and backpropagation
5.3 Introduction to deep learning
5.4 Concepts on recurrent and generative neural networks

fields.name: AI Applications
fields.description:
6.1 Expert systems: Characteristics, architecture, development and various applications
6.2 NLP: Level of analysis and application
6.3 Robotics and computer vision: Fundamental, components and applications
6.4 VLSI testing, fault diagnosis, and sustainable systems

fields.name: AI Ethics and Recent Trends
fields.description:
7.1 Responsible AI: Bias, fairness, explainability

7.2 Ethical and societal considerations

7.3 Privacy and security in AI

7.4 Emerging trends: Federated learning, embedded AI

fields.name: C Programming
fields.description: The primary goal of this course is to provide students with a solid foundation in the principles of programming and to impart practical skills in the C programming language. This course ensures that students comprehend the fundamental concepts of variables, data types, control structures, and functions within the context of C. Advanced topics such as pointers, structures, file handling and the standard C library are explored to broaden students' programming capabilities. Also, through project-based assessments and evaluations, students apply their knowledge to real-world scenarios, fostering creativity and project development skills.

fields.name: Introduction to Computer Programming
fields.description:
1.1 Definition of a computer program and programming language 
1.2 Types and generations of programming languages 
1.3 Problem-solving using a computer
1.3.1 Problem analysis
1.3.2 Algorithm and flowchart
1.3.3 Programming
1.3.4 Compilation, linking and execution
1.3.5 Debugging and testing
1.3.6 Documentation

fields.name: Overview of C Programming
fields.description:
2.1 Introduction to C programming
2.2 History and importance of C
2.3 C headers and library functions
2.4 Basic structure of a C program
2.5 Preprocessor directives
2.6 Tokens in C (Character set, keywords and identifiers)
2.7 Type casting (Implicit and explicit)
2.8 Data types, variables and constants
2.9 Compiler and IDE for C programming

fields.name: Operators & Expressions
fields.description:
3.1 Introduction to operators and expressions
3.2 Arithmetic, relational and logical operators
3.3 Assignment, increment and decrement operators
3.4 Conditional, bitwise and special operators
3.5 Comma operator, size of operator
3.6 Evaluation and type conversion in expressions
3.7 Operator precedence and associativity

fields.title: Introduction
fields.content:
Introduction to AI

  • Artificial Intelligence is composed of two words Artificial and Intelligence.

  • Artificial defines "man-made," and intelligence defines "thinking power",or "the ability to learn and solve problems".

    Hence Artificial Intelligence means "a man-made thinking power." 

So, we can define Artificial Intelligence (AI) as the branch of computer science by which we can create intelligent machines which can behave like a human, think like humans, and able to make decisions.

Major AI textbooks define artificial intelligence as "the study and design of intelligent agents," where an intelligent agent is a system that perceives its environment and takes actions which maximize its chances of success. John McCarthy, who coined the term in 1956, defines it as "the science and engineering of making intelligent machines,especially intelligent computer programs." 

The definitions of AI according to some text books are categorized into four approaches and are summarized in the table below:

Historically, all four approaches to A1 have been followed. Let us look at the four approaches in more detail. 

1. Acting humanly: The Turing Test approach 

This approach focuses on making machines behave like humans in actions and responses. It emphasizes imitation of human behavior, such as conversation, reasoning, and learning.

For testing intelligence Alan Turing (1950) proposed a test called as Turing test. He suggested a test based on common features that can match with the most intelligent entity - human beings.

The Turing Test

The computer is asked questions by a human interrogator.

The computer passes the test if a human interrogator, after posing some written questions, cannot tell whether the written responses come from a person or not. Programming a computer to pass,the computer need to possess the following capabilities:

a) Natural language processing - To enable it to communicate successfully in English. 

b) Knowledge representation - to store what it knows, what it hears.

c) Automated reasoning - to make use of stored information to answer questions being asked and to draw conclusions.

d) Machine learning - to adapt to new circumstances and to detect and make new predictions by finding patterns.

To pass complete Turing Test, the computer will also need

e) Computer vision - to perceive objects.

f) Robotics - to manipulate objects and to move.

 

2. Thinking Humanly : The cognitive modelling approach

This means trying to make computers think like humans do—so they can have their own kind of mind.

To say that a program thinks like a human, we must know how humans think. We can learn about human thought in three ways:

  • introspection—trying to catch our own thoughts as they go by;

  • psychological experiments—observing a person in action;

  • brain imaging—observing the brain in action.

Once we have a sufficiently precise theory of the mind, it becomes possible to express the theory as a computer program. If the program’s input–output behavior matches corresponding human behavior, it provides evidence that the theory—and the program's mechanisms—might mirror actual human cognition.

For example: Newell, Shaw and Simon developed a program called General Problem Solver (GPS) in 1959, that could solve many types of problems. It was capable of proving theorems, playing chess and solving complex puzzles.

The general problem solver (Newell and Simon, 1961), were less interested in whether their program solve problems correctly. Rather, they were more concerned with comparing the trace of its reasoning steps to trace of human subject to solve the same problems. 

 

3. Thinking rationally: The law of thought approach

This approach concentrates on machines using logic and reasoning to reach correct conclusions. It relies on formal rules and mathematical logic to make decisions.

Aristotle was one of the first to try and map out how to think correctly — how to build arguments that lead to true conclusions if you start with true facts.

His famous example is: Socrates is a man, all men are mortal, therefore Socrates is mortal.

Later, in the 1800s, logicians created a precise way to write statements about things and their relationships. By the 1960s, computers could use this language to solve any problem that had a logical solution. Some people in AI thought this was the key to building a smart machine—just give it all the facts and logic rules.

The BIG Problem with Pure Logic is that it only works if you are 100% certain about your starting information. Real life isn't like that: We are never 100% certain about most things (like "will it rain tomorrow?" or "what will the stock market do?"). We don't know the perfect "rules" for complex things like politics.

The fix is Probability. It lets us reason and make good guesses even when we're not sure. It can help a computer take incomplete information and make a smart prediction.But, even if a computer uses probability to understand the world perfectly and predict the future... that's still not enough to be "intelligent."

Why? Because intelligence isn't just about thinking — it's about doing. You need to take your understanding and use it to make good decisions and take the right actions.

Thinking + Acting = Intelligence.

 

4. Acting rationally: The rational agent approach

This approach focuses on machines acting in the best possible way to achieve goals. It emphasizes intelligent agents that choose optimal actions based on their environment and objectives.

An agent is anything that acts. In AI, it’s not just any program — it’s something that:

  • Works on its own

  • Senses its environment

  • Keeps running over time

  • Adapts to changes

  • Sets and goes after goals

A rational agent is one that tries to get the best possible results — or, when it’s uncertain, tries to get the best results on average.

The laws of thought approach says: intelligence is about making correct logical conclusions. But being a rational agent is more than that: sometimes the best action isn’t based on slow, careful thinking.
Example: Pulling your hand away from a hot stove is a reflex — it’s rational(तर्कसंगत), but not based on deep reasoning. So: correct reasoning is useful for rational action, but rational action doesn’t always require reasoning.

It’s broader than just logic. Logic is one tool; rationality can use other tools (reflexes, learning, etc.).

Perfect rationality is impossible in practice, but AI aims for it in theory doing the best with limited time/resources and tries to get as close as possible.

 

Foundation of AI

The field of AI is interdisciplinary, drawing on computer science, mathematics,

engineering, psychology, linguistics, and philosophy.

1. Philosophy

  • It is the very basic foundation of AI.

  • AI owes much of its foundation to ancient philosophical inquiries about the mind and reasoning. Questions posed by philosophers like Plato and Aristotle about the nature of knowledge, learning, and logic laid the groundwork for AI’s theoretical frameworks.

  • Philosophy defines that how can the formal rules be used to draw valid conclusions.

  • Without philosophy it is difficult to answer the following questions.

    • How formal rules be used to draw valid conclusions?

    • How does the mind arise from a physical brain?

    • Where does knowledge come from?

    • How does knowledge lead to action?

2. Mathematics & Statistics

  • Mathematics forms the bedrock of AI. Boolean algebra, probability theory, statistics, and calculus are integral in building algorithms.

 

 

  • In AI, the mathematics and statistics are mostly important for

    • Proving theorems,

    • Writing algorithms,

    • Computation,

    • Decidability,

    • Tractability,

    • Modelling uncertainty,

    • Learning form data.

  • Without philosophy it is difficult to answer the following questions.

    • What are the formal rules to draw valid conclusions?

    • What can be computed?

    • How do we reason with uncertain information?

3. Economics

It provides mathematical principles for rational decision-making. AI systems often need to choose the best action among many alternatives while using limited resources such as time, money, data, or computing power. Economics explains how to make such choices efficiently.

Concepts like utility maximization help AI decide which action gives the greatest benefit. Decision theory allows AI to make choices under uncertainty, game theory helps AI interact with other intelligent agents or competitors, and operations research supports optimization and long-term planning. These economic principles enable AI systems to act rationally, efficiently, and optimally, which is essential for intelligent behavior.

Example: Ride sharing apps use AI to set prices dynamically based on demand and supply.
This comes from economics (demand–supply theory) to maximize profit and reduce waiting time.

Navigation Apps (Google Maps) uses AI selects routes by comparing costs like time, fuel, and traffic.This is cost–benefit analysis, a core economic concept.

4. Neuroscience

AI is inspired by how the human brain processes information, learns, and makes decisions. Neuroscience studies the structure and working of the brain, especially neurons and how they communicate. AI researchers study the brain to understand how intelligence, learning, memory, and perception arise, and then try to replicate these processes in machines.

  • Neurons → Artificial Neurons

    • The brain works using billions of neurons connected through synapses.

    • AI models like artificial neural networks are directly inspired by this biological structure.

  • Learning from Experience
    Neuroscience shows that learning occurs by changing connections between neurons.
    Similarly, AI systems learn by adjusting weights in neural networks.

  • Information Processing
    The brain processes information in a parallel and distributed way. AI systems, especially deep learning models, follow the same principle.

  • Perception and Pattern Recognition
    Understanding how the brain recognizes speech, images, and movement has helped AI develop speech recognition, image recognition, and vision systems.

  • Without neuroscience it is difficult to answer the following questions.

    • How do brains process information?

5. Psycology

  • Psychology studies how humans and animals think, learn, and behave. 

  • It helps AI understand how intelligent behavior works by showing how we perceive information, form mental models, make decisions, and act. 

  • Cognitive psychology introduced the idea that the mind processes information like a computer, using internal representations to plan and solve problems—an idea that inspired knowledge-based AI agents. 

  • Behaviorism showed how learning can occur through stimulus-response patterns, while research in human–computer interaction guides AI to work effectively with humans. 

  • In short, psychology provides AI with the principles to simulate thinking, learning, and decision-making.

  • Without psychology it is difficult to answer the following questions.

    • How do humans and animals think and act?

6. Computer Science & Engineering

  • Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) provide the tools, methods, and infrastructure that make AI possible. While fields like neuroscience, psychology, and economics inspire AI, CSE turns these ideas into real working systems.

  • Algorithms, data structures, databases, programming languages,Networking and Cloud Computing, Hardware & System design and computational theory collectively enable the implementation and deployment of AI systems.

  • Without Computer Science & Engineering it is difficult to answer the following questions.

    • How can we build an efficient computer?

    • How can we store and retrieve large datasets quickly?

    • How can we implement complex algorithms efficiently?

    • How can we process data in parallel to speed up learning?

    • How can AI systems interact with users and other machines over networks?

7. Linguistics

  • Linguistics studies language structure, meaning, and use, which forms the basis for enabling AI systems to understand, generate, and interact with human language.

  • Natural Language Processing (NLP), a key area of AI, applies these principles to allow machines to process text and speech, perform translation, sentiment analysis, and question-answering.

  • AI does not rely on grammar alone; it must also consider context, semantics, and real-world knowledge, which links linguistics to knowledge representation and reasoning. For example, to answer a question like “Who is the president of Nepal?” an AI system needs to understand not just the words but the meaning, relationships, and current knowledge.

  • Furthermore, linguistics informs AI in areas such as:

    • Syntax and grammar rules → enabling sentence parsing and generation.

    • Semantics → understanding the meaning of words and sentences.

    • Pragmatics and context → interpreting intent, ambiguity, and implied meaning

    • Discourse and dialogue modeling → enabling chatbots and virtual assistants to hold coherent conversations.

Hence, linguistics provides AI with the framework to bridge human language and machine reasoning, making intelligent communication possible.

 

History of AI

Foundations of AI (1943–1956)

• 1943: early beginnings – McCulloch & Pitts: Boolean circuit model of brain 

• 1949: Introduced Hebbian learning and Built the first neural network computer (SNARC).

• 1950: Turing test Proposed

•1950s: initial promise – Early AI programs, including – Samuel's checkers program – Newell & Simon's Logic Theorist 

• 1956: birth of AI – Dartmouth Conference: "Artificial Intelligence―name adopted 

 

Early Enthusiasm and Symbolic AI (1952–1969)

•  Focus on games, puzzles, theorem proving, symbolic reasoning.

•  Logic Theorist (Newell & Simon): First AI program to prove mathematical theorems

•  General Problem Solver (GPS): Modeled human problem-solving behavior

•  Physical Symbol System Hypothesis: Intelligence arises from symbol manipulation

•  Arthur Samuel: Checkers program using reinforcement learning

•  McCarthy: invention of LISP, the dominant AI programming language.

•  Early Neural Networks: Perceptron (Rosenblatt), ADALINE (Widrow)

 

First AI Setback – Reality Check (1966–1973)

•  Recognition of limitations of symbolic AI and early neural networks.

•  AI systems failed on real-world problems.

•  Computational complexity and scalability issues emerged.

•  Minsky & Papert (1969) showed limitations of perceptrons.

•  Funding cuts led to the first AI winter.

•  Neural network research largely declined.

 

Expert Systems Era (1969–1986)

•  Adding domain knowledge – Development of knowledge-based systems

•  Success of rule-based expert systems, • E.g., DENDRAL (Chemical structure analysis (first expert system)), MYCIN(Medical diagnosis)

•  But were brittle and did not scale well in practice.

•  Led to another AI winter in the late 1980s.

 

Return of Neural Networks (1986–present)

•  1986: Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams popularized backpropagation.

•  Neural networks regained interest.

•  Capable of learning patterns from data.

•  More robust to noise and incomplete information.

 

Probabilistic Reasoning & Machine Learning (1987–present)

•  Shift from logic to probability and statistics

•  Judea Pearl (1988): Bayesian Networks

•  Reinforcement Learning: Linked to Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)

• Standard datasets and benchmarks emerged: ImageNet, MNIST, SQuAD, LibriSpeech

 

Big Data Revolution (2001–present)

•  Explosion of digital data due to the web and social media.

•  Performance improved more by data size than algorithm tweaks

•  Enabled large-scale learning and commercial AI revival

 

Deep Learning Era (2011–present)

•  Multi-layer neural networks

•  Key breakthroughs: ImageNet (2012), Speech recognition, Machine translation(Attention networks)

•  Self-learning through reinforcement learning

•  Enabled by: GPUs/TPUs, Massive data, Improved optimization techniques


 

AI Tree – Branches & Interdisciplinary Nature 

 

  1. Machine Learning (ML) : 

    1. Supervised Learning (e.g., classification, regression)

    2. Unsupervised Learning (e.g., clustering)

    3. Reinforcement Learning (learning through rewards) 

  2. Deep Learning (DL) : Image recognition, speech recognition, autonomous vehicles

  3. Natural Language Processing (NLP) : Text classification, Machine translation, Sentiment analysis, Question answering

  4. Computer Vision: Face recognition, Object detection, Medical image analysis

  5. Robotics : Industrial robots, drones, humanoid robots

  6. Expert Systems: Expert Systems are AI programs that simulate the decision-making ability of human experts.  Examples: Medical diagnosis systems, troubleshooting systems

  7. Knowledge Representation & Reasoning: Logic (Propositional, Predicate), Ontologies, Semantic networks. It enable machines to reason and draw conclusions.

  8. Planning & Search: 

    1. State-space search (BFS, DFS, A*), Game playing, Automated planning

    2. Example: Route Planning, Chess games 

  9. Speech Recognition: used in virtual assistants, voice-operated devices, and transcription services.

 

Interdisciplinary Nature of AI

  1. AI and Computer Science & Engineering

  2. AI and Mathematics

  3. AI and Statistics & Probability

  4. AI and Neuroscience

  5. AI and Psychology & Cognitive Science

  6. AI and Linguistics

  7. AI and Philosophy

  8. AI and Ethics

  9. AI and Economics

 

Data, Information and Knowledge

Data: Raw, unprocessed facts and figures without context. Data alone does not convey meaning.

Information: Processed or organized data that has context and meaning. Information answers the question “What does the data mean?”

Knowledge: Knowledge is organized, structured, and meaningful information that can be used by an intelligent agent to reason, make decisions, and solve problems.

In AI, knowledge represents facts, rules, relationships, and heuristics about the real world.

Knowledge answers “How can we use this information?”

Wisdom: Ability to apply knowledge judiciously, considering long-term consequences and ethical implications. Wisdom Answers “Why should we do it this way?” or “What is the best course of action?”

 

Importance of Knowledge in AI

  • Knowledge allows AI systems to select the best action i.e Decision Making

  • Problem Solving: Knowledge enables AI to:

    • Understand the problem

    • Apply rules and heuristics

    • Reach a solution efficiently

Hence, Without knowledge, AI behaves like blind trial-and-error.

 

Learning in AI

Learning is the ability of an AI system to improve its performance over time by acquiring knowledge from data or experience.

AI enables systems to learn patterns from data and make predictions or decisions without explicit programming. Whether it can learn with labeled data, learn with unlabeled data, learning through interaction with environment

Hence, “Knowledge is static information, while learning is the dynamic process of acquiring or improving knowledge.”

 

Human intelligence and machine intelligence 


 


 

Intelligent agents and types 

 

An agent is anything that can perceive its environment through sensors and act upon it through actuators.

It does not need to be intelligent; it could be simple and follow fixed rules.

  1. Example: Mechanical door with push button → reacts when button is pressed.

 

An intelligent agent is an agent that acts rationally to achieve its goals, possibly adapting to changes in the environment.

It uses knowledge, reasoning, learning, or planning to make better decisions.

Example:

  1. A self-driving car deciding the best route and speed.

  2. A robot vacuum that plans paths efficiently and avoids obstacles.

  3. A chess-playing program planning moves to win.

 

Agent–Environment Interaction

 

 

An agent can be anything. A human agent has eyes, ears, and other organs for sensors and hands, legs, vocal tract, and so on for actuators. A robotic agent might have cameras and infrared range finders for sensors and various motors for actuators. A software agent receives file contents, network packets, and human input (keyboard/mouse/touchscreen/voice) as sensory inputs and acts on the environment by writing files, sending network packets, and displaying information or generating sounds.

 

 

The environment could be everything—the entire universe! In practice it is just that part of the universe whose state we care about when designing this agent—the part that affects what the agent perceives and that is affected by the agent’s actions

We use the term percept to refer to the content an agent’s sensors are perceiving. An agent’s percept sequence is the complete history of everything the agent has ever perceived. 

 

Sensors are devices that perceive the environment and provide input data to the agent. Sensors can be physical sensors like cameras, microphones, or temperature sensors, or software-based interfaces for collecting data from digital sources, such as databases or web APIs.

 

Actuators are responsible for taking actions in the agent's environment based on the information and data received through sensors. Examples of actuators include motors, speakers, displays, and software components that can control other software or hardware devices.

 

Agent function is a map from percept sequence to an action. This component is where the agent's intelligence resides, as it involves algorithms and mechanisms for problem-solving, planning, and learning.

 

PEAS description of Agents

PEAS is a framework to describe an agent in terms of its Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, and Sensors.

 

  1. Performance Measure (P): Defines how the agent’s success is evaluated.

  2. Environment (E): The world in which the agent operates.

  3. Actuators (A): The mechanisms through which the agent acts on the environment.

  4. Sensors (S): The mechanisms through which the agent perceives the environment.

 

Let us consider a more Simple problem: Vacuum Cleaning Robot

Performance Measure: Cleaning, Efficiency, BatteryLife, Saftey, Avoiding damage to furniture.

Environment: Room, Furniture, Walls, Carpets, Other surface

Actuators: Wheels (for movement), brushes (for cleaning), suction motor/pipes, indicators (lights or sounds)

Sensors: Dirt sensor, Obstacle sensor, Cliff sensors to avoid stairs, Battery sensor

 

Let us consider a more complex problem: an automated taxi driver. 

Performance Measure: Safe, fast travel, obey traffic laws, comfortable trip, maximize profits, minimize impact on other road users

Environment: Roads, other traffic, police, pedestrians, customers, weather

Actuators: Steering, accelerator, brake, signal, horn, display, speech system

Sensors: Cameras, radar, speedometer, GPS, engine sensors, accelerometer, microphones, touchscreen

 

More Examples:

 

 

 

 


 

Vacuum Cleaning Robot: A simple Agent

The vacuum-cleaner world, consists of a robotic vacuum-cleaning agent in a world consisting of squares that can be either dirty or clean. Figure shows a configuration with just two squares, A and B. 

 

 

The vacuum agent perceives which square it is in and whether there is dirt in the square. The agent starts in square A. The available actions are to move to the right, move to the left, suck up the dirt, or do nothing.

One very simple agent function is the following: if the current square is dirty, then suck; otherwise, move to the other square.

Environment: Square A and Square B

Percepts: e.g {Location & Content] e.g.{A, dirty}

Actions: Left, Right, Suck and No operation

Goal: Check whether all square are clean

Path Cost: Each step costs 1, so the path cost is the total sum of steps in path form initial state to goal state.

 

 

Transition Diagram

 


 

Environment Types:

 

  1. Fully Observable vs Partially Observable

Fully Observable:

  1. The agent has access to the complete state of the environment at each point in time.

  2. No hidden information.

  3. Example: Chess (all pieces visible).

Partially Observable:

  1. The agent has limited or noisy information about the environment.

  2. Needs memory or sensors to infer missing info.

  3. Example: Self-driving cars where Sensors may not see everything (e.g., a pedestrian behind a truck), Poker where you can’t see other players’ cards. You must guess based on probabilities.

 

  1. Deterministic vs Stochastic

Deterministic:

  1. The next state of the environment is completely determined by the current state and the agent’s action.

  2. Example: Classical board games like Tic-Tac-Toe.

Stochastic:

  1. There is uncertainty in how the environment responds.

  2. Example: Stock market prediction, weather systems.

 

  1. Episodic vs Sequential

Episodic:

  1. Each action is independent; the environment doesn’t depend on previous actions.

  2. Example: Image classification.

Sequential:

  1. Current actions affect future decisions; history matters.

  2. Example: Chess, self-driving cars.

 

  1. Static vs Dynamic

Static:

  1. The environment does not change while the agent is thinking.

  2. Example: Crossword puzzle.

Dynamic:

  1. The environment changes over time, even if the agent does nothing.

Example: Real-time strategy games, traffic.

Semi-dynamic:

  1. Environment doesn’t change by itself but the performance measure may change.

  2. Example: A queueing system where the queue doesn’t change by itself, but waiting too long affects performance.

 

  1.  Discrete vs Continuous

Discrete:

  1. A finite number of states, actions, and time steps.

  2. Example: Board games.

Continuous:

  1. The environment has infinite possibilities for states, actions, or time.

  2. Example: Driving, robotic arm movement.

 

  1. Single-agent vs Multi-agent

Single-agent:

  1. Only one agent acts in the environment.

  2. Example: Maze solving.

Multi-agent:

  1. Multiple agents interact, can be cooperative or competitive.

  2. Example: Soccer simulation, online multiplayer games.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Types of Agents:

  1. Simple reflex agents;

  2. Model-based reflex agents;

  3. Goal-based agents; and

  4. Utility-based agents.

 

  1. Simple reflex agents

  2. These agents select actions on the basis of the current percept, ignoring the rest of the percept history.

  3. Works on condition-action rules: If condition then action.

  4. The agent will work only if the correct decision can be made on the basis of only the current percept - work only if the environment is fully observable.

 

 

 

  1. Model-Based Reflex Agent

  2. The most effective way to handle partial observability is to keep track of the part of the world it can’t see now. 

 

 

 

  1. That is, the agent should maintain some sort of internal state that depends on the percept history and thereby reflects at least some of the unobserved aspects of the current state. 

  2. This can be done using two kinds of knowledge:- 

    1. how the world evolves independently of the agent 

      1. how the agent's own actions affect the world

 

 

 

 

  1. Goal-Based Agent 

  2. Knowing the current state of the environment is not enough. The agent needs some goal information.

  3.  For example, at a road junction, the taxi can turn left, turn right, or go straight on. The correct decision depends on where the taxi is trying to get to. In other words, as well as a current state description, the agent needs some sort of goal information that describes situations that are desirable—for example, being at a particular destination. 

 

 

  1. Utility-Based Agent · 

  2. Sometimes achieving the desired goal is not enough. We may look for quicker, safer, cheaper trip to reach a destination.

  3. Agent happiness should be taken into consideration. We call it utility. · 

  4. A utility function is the agent’s performance measure. · 

  5. Because of the uncertainty in the world, a utility agent chooses the action that maximizes the expected utility.

 

 

 

Learning Agents:

 

 

 

A learning agent can be divided into four conceptual components · 

  1. Learning element: responsible for making improvements. · 

  2. Performance element: responsible for selecting external actions. · 

  3. Critic: how well the agent is doing w.r.t. a fixed performance standard. · 

  4. Problem generator: allows the agent to explore.

 

 

 

Application of AI

 


 

Assignment:

  1. Define Artificial Intelligence. Explain the different approaches of AI.

  2. Define knowledge and explain its role in AI.

  3. Define learning in the context of AI and why learning is crucial for intelligent behavior.

  4. Compare human intelligence and machine intelligence.

  5. Define an intelligent agent in AI. Explain different types of agents with block diagram.

  6. Discuss the foundational disciplines of AI and how each contributes to building intelligent systems.

  7. Explain the PEAS framework. For each provide examples of an agent write (Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors).

  8. Personal Assistant(Siri)

  9. Industrial Robotic Arm

  10. Smart Home Security System

  11. Tutor

fields.title: SOCIAL DISTANCING VIOLATION DETECTION
